metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for colonoscopy after positive FIT in patient with HFrEF (EF 25%) and ICD

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no anticoagulant on GI med list; MedicationRequests limited to sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone, ASA 81
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: cardiology started apixaban 5 mg twice daily ~6 weeks ago for newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation
planted_discrepancies.note: GI chart is stale; a therapeutic-dose DOAC not on file materially changes periprocedural bleeding management and hold/bridge planning for colonoscopy with anticipated polypectomy

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no atrial fibrillation documented in GI problem list
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: new-onset atrial fibrillation diagnosed by cardiology ~6 weeks ago, reason apixaban was started
planted_discrepancies.note: explains the new anticoagulant; also relevant to periprocedural rhythm/rate management

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: HFrEF EF 25%, ischemic cardiomyopathy, ICD placed 2024; referral flags mandatory anesthesia review and device management plan (magnet vs reprogram)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms ICD in place, follows with EP, EF unchanged; no decompensation currently
planted_discrepancies.note: RED tier is correct and must not be downgraded — anesthesia clearance and ICD peri-procedure management remain mandatory

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: cardiology halved it to 12.5 mg daily ~1 month ago after a high potassium; patient taking the lower dose
planted_discrepancies.note: dose on chart is stale; relevant to periprocedural electrolytes and bowel-prep-related potassium shifts

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms daily baby aspirin; distinct from the newly added apixaban — she lumps both together as 'my blood thinners'
planted_discrepancies.note: the meaningful trap is separating cardioprotective low-dose ASA (often continued) from therapeutic apixaban (D1); do not conflate them

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms taking both daily as prescribed

prior_notes.document_type: Primary care referral / GI intake note
prior_notes.text: 58-year-old woman referred to GI for positive fecal immunochemical test on routine screening. Asymptomatic — denies overt rectal bleeding, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. Significant cardiac history: ischemic cardiomyopathy with HFrEF, most recent EF 25%, single-chamber ICD placed 2024, CAD. Cardiac meds: sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone, low-dose aspirin. Given EF 25% and ICD, anesthesia and device management input will be required prior to any procedure. Referring for diagnostic colonoscopy.

Ischemic cardiomyopathy, HFrEF EF 25%, ICD (single chamber) implanted 2024, functioning normally at last interrogation. New this visit: patient found to be in atrial fibrillation on routine ECG, rate-controlled, CHA2DS2-VASc elevated — started apixaban 5 mg BID for stroke prophylaxis. Also noted potassium 5.4 on labs; spironolactone reduced from 25 mg to 12.5 mg daily and repeat BMP ordered. Continue carvedilol and sacubitril/valsartan. Return in 3 months; ICD interrogation and periprocedural device plan to be provided if she proceeds with any surgery/endoscopy.

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Severely reduced LV systolic function, EF 25%. Regional wall motion abnormalities consistent with prior ischemic injury. No significant valvular disease. Findings unchanged from prior.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Al-Rashid. Thanks for coming in. Your stool screening test came back positive, so we want to plan a colonoscopy to take a proper look. Before anything, I want to go carefully through your heart history and your medicines, because there's a lot going on there.
MA: Before the doctor goes on, let me get your vitals — you're 5 foot 4 and 150 pounds today. Temp 36.8, heart rate 60, breathing 14, blood pressure 118 over 72, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes, my heart keeps everyone busy. I have the weak heart — they told me the pumping is about twenty-five percent — and I have the defibrillator, the device in my chest, since 2024.
DR: That's exactly what I have here. And you follow with your heart rhythm doctor for the device?
PT: Every few months. They checked it in the spring, said it was working fine.
DR: Good. Any chest pain, more shortness of breath, swelling, or fainting lately?
PT: No, actually I've been pretty steady. No worse than usual.
DR: That's reassuring. Now the medicine list I have is the Entresto, the carvedilol, spironolactone, and a baby aspirin. Is that still right?
PT: Mostly. The Entresto and carvedilol, yes, every day. But the water pill — the spironolactone — the cardiologist cut that in half last month. My potassium ran high, so I'm on the twelve-and-a-half now, not the twenty-five.
DR: Thank you, that's important — I'll correct that. And the aspirin?
PT: Yes, the little aspirin every day. And now the new blood thinner too.
DR: Wait — a new blood thinner? Tell me about that.
PT: About six weeks ago they found my heart was in that irregular rhythm — the atrial fibrillation. So they started me on apixaban, the twice-a-day one. I take that with the aspirin. I just think of them both as my blood thinners.
DR: I'm really glad you mentioned it, because that changes how we prepare you. The aspirin and the apixaban are two different things — the apixaban especially, we'll need to plan when to hold it around the procedure, and coordinate with your heart team.
PT: I see. I didn't realize it mattered that much.
DR: It matters a lot. Here's the plan: we'll schedule the colonoscopy, but first I'll get anesthesia to review you because of your heart, and your device doctor will give us a plan for the defibrillator during the procedure — usually a magnet or a reprogram. And I'll write out exactly which days to hold the apixaban. The aspirin we'll likely continue.
PT: Okay. That makes me feel better that you're checking all of it.
DR: That's the job. The team will call you with the prep and the medication schedule.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Al-Rashid is a 58-year-old woman referred to GI for a positive screening FIT (2026-06-28). She is asymptomatic from a GI standpoint — denies overt rectal bleeding, melena, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. Extensive cardiac history: ischemic cardiomyopathy with HFrEF (EF 25% on 2026-04 echo), single-chamber ICD implanted 2024, CAD. She denies current chest pain, worsening dyspnea, edema, or syncope and reports being at her cardiac baseline.

Medication reconciliation reveals two chart discrepancies. (1) NEW, not on the GI chart: she was started on apixaban 5 mg twice daily approximately 6 weeks ago by cardiology for newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation. She refers to both her aspirin and apixaban as her "blood thinners." (2) Spironolactone was reduced by cardiology from 25 mg to 12.5 mg daily ~1 month ago for hyperkalemia (K 5.4); she is taking the lower dose. She confirms ongoing sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, and low-dose aspirin 81 mg.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 60, RR 14, BP 118/72, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 68 kg (150 lb), BMI 25.6. Recent data — LVEF 25% (echo 2026-04-30); NT-proBNP 1420 pg/mL, K 5.4 mmol/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, Hgb 12.6 g/dL (2026-06-05); FIT positive (2026-06-28). Single-chamber ICD in situ, last interrogation spring 2026 reported normal. Appears euvolemic and at cardiac baseline by history.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Positive FIT — plan diagnostic colonoscopy with anticipated polypectomy.
- HIGH-ACUITY cardiac status (EF 25%, ICD): RED tier confirmed. Mandatory anesthesia/pre-anesthesia review prior to procedure. Obtain EP device-management plan for periprocedural ICD (magnet vs reprogram per policy).
- NEW anticoagulation: apixaban 5 mg BID for new atrial fibrillation — NOT previously on GI chart. Plan periprocedural hold schedule for apixaban in coordination with cardiology/EP; no bridging anticipated for AFib with DOAC but confirm with cardiology given EF. Add atrial fibrillation and apixaban to problem/med list.
- Low-dose aspirin 81 mg — cardioprotective; plan to continue through colonoscopy/polypectomy. Explicitly distinguished from apixaban.
- Update med list: spironolactone 12.5 mg daily (not 25 mg); note hyperkalemia — recheck electrolytes before prep given bowel-prep-related shifts and renal function.
- Continue sacubitril/valsartan and carvedilol.
- Team to contact patient with bowel prep, anesthesia scheduling, and explicit medication hold instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your stool test (FIT) was positive, so we are planning a colonoscopy.
• Your heart history: weak heart pump (EF 25%) and your defibrillator (ICD).
• Your medicines, including a new blood thinner (apixaban) started for an irregular heartbeat.

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy. Because of your heart, anesthesia will review you first, and your heart-rhythm doctor will give a plan for your defibrillator during the procedure.
• We will send you exact instructions for when to STOP your apixaban before the procedure. Do not stop it on your own — wait for our instructions.
• You will likely keep taking your baby aspirin — we will confirm in your instructions.
• We updated your record: spironolactone is now 12.5 mg (the lower dose your cardiologist set), and we added your atrial fibrillation and apixaban.
• Keep taking your Entresto and carvedilol as usual.
• Call your care team or seek care for chest pain, severe shortness of breath, fainting, or any bleeding.
